question: Should Single-Use Plastics Be Banned?
agent1_stance:
Your stance:
You support banning single-use plastics.
You believe bans force people to snap out of convenience culture and find better alternatives, with reusable options like cotton bags and metal straws being less expensive over time.
You believe bans demonstrably work, as a January 2024 study found bans have cut single-use plastic bag consumption by about 6 billion bags per year, and plastic straw bans successfully normalized alternatives.
You believe only 5-6 percent of plastic is actually being recycled into new products in the U.S., and no type of single-use plastic food service item can legally be claimed as recyclable.
agent2_stance:
Your stance:
You oppose banning single-use plastics.
You believe single-use plastics serve critical functions, keeping food fresh, enabling medical sterility through syringes and blood-transfer bags, and assisting people with disabilities who rely upon a straw that they cannot easily wash.
You believe bans create complacency and mask a greater need—a reduction in all plastic production—as we would still release more than 17 million tons of plastic per year into nature despite consumer-side policies.
You believe bans wrongly punish consumers, 94 percent of whom support recycling, and the focus should shift toward manufacturers developing improved recycling technologies.
